---
title: "Networks with SNiGB"
format: html
---
Use this document as a starting point for practicing network analysis with the Social Network in Georgian Britain.

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
library(tidyverse)
library(igraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(ggraph)
library(here)
```

## Create the network
Load the data. We need an edge list and a node list.

```{r}
#| label: load-data
edges <- read_csv(here("data-raw", "associations.csv"))
individuals <- read_csv(here("data-raw", "individuals.csv"))
```

View the data frames using RStudio's viewer or print the data frames out to the console. Not all of the individuals are in the associates data, so let's find out who is in the associates data. Each person might be in either from `from` or the `to` column. The `unique()` function helps to find distinct names.
```{r}
#| label: nodes
node_ids <- unique(c(edges$from, edges$to))
nodes <- individuals |> 
  filter(id %in% node_ids)
```

Now we are ready to use the edges and nodes to create a graph object. For whatever reason, I find that this works much better by creating a igraph object and then converting it to a tidygraph object later.

```{r}
#| label: create-graph
assoc_graph <- graph_from_data_frame(d = edges, vertices = nodes, directed = FALSE)
```

Look at the network you have created and find the 5 number summary as discussed in the [Networks with R](https://jessesadler.github.io/vt5444s26/resources/networks.html) resource page. Some of the calculations like diameter may take a while to run on a network of this size.

```{r}
#| label: 5-nr-summary
# Investigate graph: 5 number summary
# 1. Size
gorder(assoc_graph)
# 2. Density (0-1)
edge_density(assoc_graph)
# 3. Components: only one, all nodes have at least one edge
count_components(assoc_graph)
# 4. Diameter
diameter(assoc_graph)
# 5. Clustering coefficient (0-1)
transitivity(assoc_graph)
```

You will notice that the network has many different components; there are small clusters of connected people that are completely unconnected to the largest network. We can remove these eccentricities and concentrate on the large network with `largest_component()` from igraph.

```{r}
#| label: largest-component
assoc_graph <- largest_component(assoc_graph)
```

Now rerun the five-number summary on the new network.

```{r}
#| label: 5-nr-summary-comp
# Investigate graph: 5 number summary
# 1. Size
gorder(assoc_graph)
# 2. Density (0-1)
edge_density(assoc_graph)
# 3. Components: only one, all nodes have at least one edge
count_components(assoc_graph)
# 4. Diameter
diameter(assoc_graph)
# 5. Clustering coefficient (0-1)
transitivity(assoc_graph)
```


Let's also make a tidygraph object so we can see how these two interfaces differ.

```{r}
#| label: make-tidygraph
assoc_tbl <- as_tbl_graph(assoc_graph)
assoc_tbl
```

`assoc_tbl` and `assoc_graph` are equivalent. They differ in how they represent the data. the tidygraph interface will be more familiar even if it consists of two data frames. One advantage is that it allows us to concentrate on either the nodes or edges data frame and plot aspects of one or the other with ggplot2. For instance, another way to get an overview of the nature of the network as a whole is to look at the distribution of the number of connections between nodes. It is typical in networks for many nodes to only have a few connections and for a small number of nodes to have a lot of connections.

```{r}
#| label: plot-degree
assoc_tbl |> 
  mutate(degree = centrality_degree()) |> # calculate number of connections
  as_tibble() |> # only get the nodes tibble
  ggplot(aes(x = degree)) + # Plot
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) + 
  scale_x_log10() + # Use log scale for x axis
  theme_bw()
```

There are many different [centrality measures](https://tidygraph.data-imaginist.com/reference/index.html#centrality) in tidygrapgh. Play around with different ones and see what they do.
centrality_katz(alpha = NULL)
centrality_power(exponent = 1, rescale = FALSE, tol = 1e-07, loops = FALSE)
### Add id column
You will notice that the tidygraph represents the nodes in the edge tibble as integers. It will make it easier to identify the names associated with these values if the node tibble also has these values. The values are calculated in sequential order of the nodes, so we can add it as a column with `mutate()`.

```{r}
#| label: add-id
assoc_tbl <- assoc_tbl |> 
  mutate(node_id = seq_along(assoc_tbl)) |> # Add id column from 1 to end of the tbl
  select(node_id, everything()) # Move the id column to the front
```

Let's now look at a couple of different ways to explore connections in the graph.

## Subgraphs
You can create subgraphs using `filter()`. For instance, if you are interested in the connections between women in the data you might create a subgraph of women.

```{r}
#| label: subgraph
female_graph <- assoc_tbl |> 
  filter(sex == "female")
female_graph
```

You will notice that there are over 1,000 components, which tells us something about how men connect women together in this data. Explore this graph or create your own subgraph to investigate the data.

```{r}
#| label: explore-subgraphs
female_graph <- assoc_tbl |> 
  filter(sex == "female")
female_graph

#| label: subgraph
male_graph <- assoc_tbl |> 
  filter(sex == "male")
male_graph

#| label: subgraph
london_graph <- assoc_tbl |> 
  filter(location == "London, England")
london_graph
plot(largest_component(london_graph), vertex.label = NA, vertex.size = 3)
london_female <- assoc_tbl|>
  filter(location == "London, England", sex == "female")

assoc_tbl|>
  filter(sex == "female")

```


## Ego graphs
Neighborhoods or ego graphs provide a way to create a subgraph based on the connection to one or more node. Find an individual to investigate. One way is to view the `nodes` data frame by clicking on it in RStudio to explore the individuals in the network. You can either use the row number in the data viewer or do a filter for their name in `assoc_tbl`. One trick to do this is to use `str_starts()` within `filter()`. This lets you write the beginning of the name without having to write everything exactly perfectly. For instance:

```{r}
#| label: find-individual
assoc_tbl |> 
  filter(str_starts(name, "Jane Austen")) |> 
  pull(node_id) # only get values from id column
```

This shows the `node_id` for Jane Austen, which can be used to make the ego graph. Assign the id value of the person you choose to `individual_1` below. By using a variable, you will only need to change the `node_id` in one place if you want to explore another individual.

```{r}
#| label: ego
individual_1 <- 7550 # this is Jane Austen
to_local_neighborhood(assoc_tbl, node = individual_1)
```

Within tidygraph, this creates a temporary representation of the graph. Notice the `$neighborhood` at the beginning of the print out of the object. This is because `to_local_neighborhood()` actually returns a list. Run `class(to_local_neighborhood(assoc_tbl, node = individual_1))` to confirm this. If you want to actually create a new tidygraph object, you need to use `convert()` with the graph object and the function used for the conversion as arguments in the following manner:

```{r}
#| label: tidy-convert

convert(assoc_tbl, to_local_neighborhood, node = individual_1)
```

Make sure to assign the above to a variable so that you can use it below.

See the [documentation](https://tidygraph.data-imaginist.com/reference/morph.html) for a fuller explanation of using `convert()`.

You can now plot with either the igraph version of `plot()` or use ggraph. Here is the basic plot. If the labels are distracting, you can remove them using `vertex.label = NA`. You can also make the size of the nodes smaller with `vertex.size`. See the [igraph plotting documentation](https://r.igraph.org/reference/plot.common.html) for more details. 

```{r}
#| label: base-ego-plot
plot(ego_graph)
```

Try to create a plot with ggraph following the instructions in [visualize the graph](https://jessesadler.github.io/vt5444s26/resources/networks.html#visualize-the-graph).

```{r}
#| label: ego-ggraph
# place graph object in first position
ggraph(, layout = "nicely") + 
  geom_edge_link() + 
  geom_node_point() + 
  theme_graph()
```

## Shortest paths between nodes
Another way to investigate the graph is to look at how two people are connected to each other. This can be done by finding the shortest paths between two nodes. Find an individual in the data who is not a neighbor (someone who is not directly connected) of the individual you chose above. Unfortunately, the process to do this is a bit more complicated than we might want. It demonstrates how we can go back and forth between tidygraph and igraph.

The tidygraph function `to_shortest_path()` only provides a single path between nodes even when there may be multiple paths that are the same distance. igraph's `all_shortest_paths()` provides this information, but it returns a complicated list in which the first element contains the various paths between the nodes. One way to deal with this is to get a vector of all the nodes and then create a subgraph with only those nodes.

```{r}
#| label: shortest-paths
individual_2 <- 725
# This is fairly complex, but you can try running one line at a time
# to see what each function does.
all_paths <- assoc_tbl |> 
  all_shortest_paths(from = individual_1, to = individual_2) |> 
  first() |> # choose first element of the list
  list_c() |> # convert the list to a vector
  unique() |> # reduce the vector to the unique nodes
  as_ids() # convert from igraph vertices to character vector of names

# Create the subgraph
all_paths_subgraph <- assoc_tbl |> 
  filter(name %in% all_paths)
```

You can now calculate aspects of the graph or of the different nodes on this subgraph or create a plot.

```{r}
#| label: shortest-paths-experiment

```


## Conclusion
There are many other ways to explore this network data. Make sure to look through the [igraph](https://r.igraph.org/index.html) and [tidygraph](https://tidygraph.data-imaginist.com/index.html) documentation for ideas.
